sourceTitle: Project management that evolves with your team

questions.question: Why does Notion recommend using a Status property in a Projects database?

options.text: It automatically assigns tasks to team members.
options.explanation: Status tracks the stage of a project, not task assignment.

options.text: It lets you customize workflow steps and visualize progress on a board.
options.explanation: A Status property defines each step in your workflow and, when used with a Board view, lets you drag cards to update progress.

options.text: It creates a timeline automatically based on start dates.
options.explanation: Timelines are built from Date properties, not Status.

options.text: It replaces the need for a Person property.
options.explanation: Status and Person serve different purposes; Person assigns owners.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of linking a Tasks database to a Content Calendar via a Relation property?

options.text: It duplicates the task records so each calendar entry has its own copy.
options.explanation: Relation links records, it does not duplicate them.

options.text: Updates made in one place (e.g., marking a task as Done) automatically reflect in the related project.
options.explanation: A Relation ensures changes propagate, keeping the project status in sync with task completion.

options.text: It automatically generates a Gantt chart for the entire project.
options.explanation: Gantt charts require a Timeline view, not just a Relation.

options.text: It prevents any filters from being applied to the tasks.
options.explanation: Relations do not affect filter capabilities; you can still filter linked records.

questions.question: How can you visually prioritize projects using Notion’s built‑in features?

options.text: By adding a Select property with priority tags like P0, P1, etc.
options.explanation: Select lets you tag each project with a priority level, which can then be used for sorting or filtering.

options.text: By changing the color of the page background for each project.
options.explanation: Page background colors are not a native way to set priority.

options.text: By moving the project card to the top of the board manually each day.
options.explanation: Manual repositioning is not systematic; priority tags enable automated sorting.

options.text: By creating a separate database for high‑priority projects.
options.explanation: While possible, it’s unnecessary when a Select property provides built‑in prioritization.

questions.question: Which Notion view would you choose to see a project’s schedule over months and years?

options.text: Board view grouped by Status
options.explanation: Board view is great for status columns, not for a time‑based schedule.

options.text: Timeline view with start and end dates
options.explanation: Timeline view displays items along a calendar axis using Date properties.

options.text: Table view with a filtered Status column
options.explanation: Table view shows rows but does not provide a visual time line.

options.text: Gallery view sorted by Priority
options.explanation: Gallery view shows cards as thumbnails; it isn’t a time‑based representation.

questions.question: What does a Rollup property allow you to display in a Projects database?

options.text: A list of all users who have commented on the project page.
options.explanation: Rollup aggregates data from related records, not comments.

options.text: The percentage of completed tasks for each project.
options.explanation: Rollup can calculate aggregates such as count of completed tasks, enabling a progress bar or ring.

options.text: The raw JSON of the related task entries.
options.explanation: Rollup does not expose raw JSON; it returns calculated values.

options.text: A dynamic AI‑generated summary of the project description.
options.explanation: AI Summary is a separate property; Rollup works on relational data.

questions.question: How does embedding external content (e.g., Figma files) improve collaboration in Notion?

options.text: It converts the external file into a native Notion block that can be edited directly.
options.explanation: Embedding displays the file; editing still occurs in the original tool.

options.text: It lets team members view the content without leaving the Notion workspace, keeping context unified.
options.explanation: Embedding brings external assets into Notion pages, reducing context switching.

options.text: It automatically syncs changes made in Notion back to the original external tool.
options.explanation: Embedding is a live view; changes must be made in the source tool.

options.text: It replaces the need for a separate project management tool entirely.
options.explanation: Embedding enhances Notion but doesn’t eliminate all specialized tool needs.

questions.question: Which property type would you use to automatically generate a brief overview of a project's current state?

options.text: Formula
options.explanation: Formulas calculate based on other fields but don’t produce natural‑language summaries.

options.text: Relation
options.explanation: Relation links databases; it doesn’t generate text.

options.text: AI Summary
options.explanation: AI Summary property uses Notion AI to create a dynamic text summary that updates as the project changes.

options.text: Select
options.explanation: Select offers predefined choices, not generated summaries.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a database template in a project tracker?

options.text: To lock the page so no one can edit it.
options.explanation: Templates provide a starting structure, not edit restrictions.

options.text: To pre‑populate a new page with consistent blocks, properties, and layout, speeding up setup.
options.explanation: Templates ensure every new project or task starts with the same structure, saving time.

options.text: To create a backup copy of the entire database.
options.explanation: Templates are for individual records, not full backups.

options.text: To automatically assign a random owner to each new project.
options.explanation: Owner assignment requires a Person property or automation, not a template alone.

questions.question: How can you ensure that only high‑priority tasks appear on a team’s dashboard view?

options.text: Create a filter on the tasks view that shows only items where the Priority Select property equals “P0”.
options.explanation: Filtering by the Priority Select property restricts the view to high‑priority tasks.

options.text: Change the color of the task cards to red.
options.explanation: Color changes are visual only and don’t affect which items appear in a view.

options.text: Move the tasks database to a separate page.
options.explanation: Location doesn’t control visibility; filters do.

options.text: Rename the tasks database to include the word “High”.
options.explanation: Renaming doesn’t filter content; it only changes the database title.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the benefit of using multiple saved views for a single Notion database?

options.text: It creates duplicate databases that can be edited independently.
options.explanation: Saved views are just different presentations of the same underlying data, not duplicates.

options.text: It allows the same data to be displayed in different layouts (e.g., board, timeline, table) for various team needs.
options.explanation: Multiple views let users switch between board, timeline, table, etc., without altering the data.

options.text: It automatically syncs data to external tools like Jira.
options.explanation: Syncing to external tools requires separate integrations, not just saved views.

options.text: It locks the database schema so properties cannot be changed.
options.explanation: Views don’t affect schema editability; they only change presentation.
